parts.text: A concurrency library provides a signal primitive for semaphores and a notify primitive for condition variables. Explain what they have in common and how they are different. Are they blocking primitives? Can they have no effect?
Without using an example, define the notion of serialisability of transactions.
What is a conflict between two threads, and can conflicts be used to check for serialisability?
What are the advantages and disadvantages of non-strict isolation?
Can performance be enhanced when increment and decrement operations are considered as composite operations? Consider this in the context of conflict analysis techniques.
Give an example, not using increments or decrements, where simplistic conflict analysis may report a problem that in reality is not a problem.

Some code is replaced in an asynchronous, reliable message-passing language that uses channel_id!value and ?channel_id to communicate between user-space threads. The whole system has now seized up. Sketch two possible code fragments that may have caused the seizure, one due to deadlock, and one not due to deadlock.
Three nodes N1...N3 communicate using point-to-point messages. Give Lamport timestamps for each of the events e1...e9, assuming clocks start from 0.
What is a necessary condition on Lamport timestamps for an event x to have happened before an event y? Is this a sufficient condition? Briefly justify your answer based on the events in the example.
Now give possible vector timestamps for each of the events e1...e9.
What is a necessary and sufficient condition on vector timestamps for an event x to have happened before an event y?

Write pseudo-code to implement causal broadcast, assuming access to an underlying reliable broadcast protocol. Describe the local state held at each node along with the algorithm used to broadcast a message, and to decide when a message can be delivered locally.
A colleague suggests that the space required by vector timestamps makes this algorithm inefficient. They suggest basing the algorithm on Lamport timestamps instead. Do you agree with this suggestion?

A guarded resource is locked by readers, moderators, and writers. Any number of readers may hold it concurrently, up to two moderators, or at most one writer. Define the locks, mutexes or shared variables needed, and sketch a state transition diagram.
Using a monitor approach, give pseudocode for: start_write(), start_moderate(), start_read(), end_write(), end_moderate(), end_read().
Describe two deadlock avoidance techniques, one graceful deadlock recovery technique (not reboot), and two burdensome aspects of deadlock recovery.
A system has T threads and 3 resource types with 4 instances each. What state space does the system have before and after applying a deadlock avoidance restriction?
Define fair-loss network links.
Define crash-recovery execution.
Define asynchronous timing model.
A traffic monitoring system uses quorum-based replication with 5 nodes, quorum size 3. Write pseudocode for setCount(n) — update local counter and replicate to a quorum.
Write pseudocode for getTotalCount() — return the total of all nodes' counters.
Does your system provide linearizable behaviour? Explain or give a counterexample.
A second version requires strong eventual consistency, always completing regardless of available nodes. Summarise the changes needed.
A shared resource allows one writer or concurrent readers. What design choices exist regarding fairness?
Compare two simple locks (read/write) vs a single specialised locking primitive. What additional state transitions could the specialised lock support?
If deadlock is avoided via locking order, is a complete or partial order needed?
How should specialised lock transitions integrate into a lock ordering policy?
A mutex is acquired at the start of a function body. What can go wrong if the function is recursive?
A friend suggests allowing re-acquisition if the same thread already holds the lock. Could this work and is it worthwhile?
Give pseudocode for emulating a shared global variable in a message-passing system.
Discuss whether emulating locking primitives such as semaphores in a message-passing system is possible or worthwhile.

A social network stores follow/unfollow relationships across 5 servers (majority quorum = 3). Using asynchronous system model, fair-loss links, crash-recovery faults and Lamport timestamps, give pseudocode for: (1) u1 follows u2; (2) u1 unfollows u2; (3) get followers of u. Describe the data structures and messages used.

When a user posts a chirp, it must be sent to all followers. Delivery order: same-author chirps in post order; replies must be delivered after the chirp they reply to. Give pseudocode using the follower operation from (a). Explain and justify your approach.
Explain the difference between a semaphore and a monitor in terms of their structure and the abstractions they provide. In what circumstances would you prefer to use a monitor over a semaphore?

(i) Define deadlock and distinguish it from livelock. (ii) A system uses four threads and four resources. Under what conditions can deadlock be avoided without requiring threads to release all resources simultaneously? (iii) Explain why circular wait is a necessary condition for deadlock in a system using only lock-based synchronization.

(i) Define strict serializability and explain how it differs from serializability. [2 marks] (ii) In a multi-versioned concurrency control system, can a read-only transaction ever cause a write transaction to abort? Justify your answer. [3 marks]

A distributed system uses logical clocks to order events across multiple nodes. Explain why logical clocks alone are insufficient for detecting causality violations in a replicated data store, and describe what additional mechanism is needed.

In a consensus protocol tolerating Byzantine faults, explain why the number of faulty nodes must be strictly less than one-third of the total. Sketch how a consensus protocol might guarantee safety even when up to ⌊(n−1)/3⌋ nodes are Byzantine.

Explain the difference between a semaphore and a monitor. In particular, discuss how their approaches to mutual exclusion and condition synchronisation differ. Which primitive is more suitable for implementing a bounded buffer, and why?

(i) Define deadlock and distinguish it from livelock. [2 marks]
(ii) Can a system with fair scheduling still suffer from deadlock? Justify your answer. [1 mark]

(i) Define strict serializability and explain how it differs from serializability. [2 marks]
(ii) Why might a system choose to implement only serializability rather than strict serializability? [2 marks]

Consider a distributed system with processes that communicate only by message passing and have no access to a global clock.
(i) Explain why Lamport logical clocks alone are insufficient to establish a total order of events across the entire system. [2 marks]
(ii) How do vector clocks improve upon Lamport clocks? Give one example scenario where vector clocks provide information that Lamport clocks cannot. [3 marks]

In a message-passing system supporting RPC, a client sends a request to a server and waits for a reply. The server crashes after receiving the request but before sending the reply.
(i) Explain what happens from the client's perspective and why this is problematic. [2 marks]
(ii) Discuss whether at-most-once semantics or at-least-once semantics is more appropriate here, and what additional mechanisms would be required. [2 marks]

Explain the difference between a semaphore and a monitor. In particular, discuss how they differ in their treatment of mutual exclusion and condition synchronization. What are the relative advantages of each approach for preventing race conditions?
(i) Define the concept of atomicity in the context of concurrent programming. What operations are typically considered atomic by a modern multiprocessor?
(ii) What is the difference between deadlock and livelock? Can a system that is livelocked ever make progress towards completing its intended task?

(iii) Consider a transaction system that uses optimistic concurrency control based on read-write conflict detection. Explain why write-write conflicts must always be detected, but read-write conflicts may sometimes be safely ignored without violating serialisability. Provide a brief justification.
(iv) What is lock-free programming, and what advantage does it offer over lock-based synchronization in terms of fault tolerance? Name one potential disadvantage.

A distributed system uses asynchronous message passing with blocking send and receive operations. Threads communicate via named channels using the syntax send(channel, message) and recv(channel). (i) [3 marks] Sketch a code fragment for two threads that will deadlock when both attempt to communicate. Explain why deadlock occurs. (ii) [2 marks] Sketch a different code fragment that causes the system to seize up but does NOT involve deadlock. What is the cause? (iii) [2 marks] Propose a simple remedy to prevent the scenario in part (ii) without fundamentally changing the message-passing model.

Explain the difference between physical clocks and logical clocks in a distributed system. Why is it necessary to use logical clocks rather than physical clocks for determining causality between events?
Define the happens-before relation (→) in the context of Lamport's logical clocks.

Consider two processes P₁ and P₂ communicating via messages. Process P₁ sends a message at Lamport time 5, and P₂ receives it. If P₂'s clock was at 3 before receiving the message, what must P₂'s clock be set to after receiving the message? Justify your answer.

In a replicated system, explain what is meant by eventual consistency. How does it differ from strong consistency? Give one practical example where eventual consistency is preferable and one where strong consistency is required.
What is the consensus problem in distributed systems? State the three key safety properties that any consensus algorithm must satisfy.

A distributed system is said to be resilient to f crash faults using a consensus algorithm. If a system has n processes and can tolerate f faults, what is the minimum value of n in terms of f? Explain why this bound exists.

Explain the difference between a semaphore and a monitor. In your answer, discuss how mutual exclusion is achieved in each, and comment on the ease of use for programmers implementing concurrent code.
(i) Define a deadlock in a distributed system and explain the four necessary conditions for deadlock to occur.
(ii) Explain why detecting deadlock in a distributed system is more challenging than in a single-process system. Consider communication delays and the global state problem.
(iii) Describe one practical deadlock avoidance strategy and explain how it prevents at least one of the necessary conditions for deadlock.

Consider a distributed system where multiple processes maintain replicas of a shared variable. Explain why using physical (wall-clock) time to order updates across processes is problematic. How does the use of logical clocks, such as Lamport clocks, improve the situation?

A client-server system communicates via RPC over an unreliable network. Describe three possible failure scenarios that can occur during an RPC call and explain the challenges in implementing exactly-once semantics. Give one practical approach to achieve at-least-once semantics and comment on whether it is sufficient for all application types.

Explain the difference between a condition variable's wait() and signal() operations. In particular, discuss whether signal() always unblocks a waiting thread, and what happens if signal() is called when no threads are waiting.

(i) [3 marks] Define strict two-phase locking (Strict 2PL) and explain why it guarantees serialisability.
(ii) [3 marks] A transaction uses optimistic concurrency control with version numbers. Explain what validation must occur before commit, and why this approach may be preferable to locking in read-heavy workloads.
(iii) [3 marks] Consider two transactions T1 and T2 that both read a value x, then both write to x. Under Strict 2PL, can this ever lead to a race condition? Under optimistic concurrency control with validation, what happens?
(iv) [3 marks] Explain the concept of a dirty read and discuss whether it can occur under Strict 2PL, under optimistic concurrency control, and under read uncommitted isolation.

In a distributed system using RPC, a client sends a request to server A, which then sends an RPC request to server B, which sends an RPC request back to server A. Explain why this pattern is prone to deadlock, and describe one practical mitigation strategy.

Explain the difference between a semaphore and a monitor in terms of their abstraction level and ease of use. Why might a programmer prefer to use a monitor-based approach when implementing a bounded buffer, compared to using semaphores directly?

(i) [2 marks] Define strict serializability and explain how it differs from serializability.

(ii) [3 marks] Two concurrent transactions T1 and T2 both read variable x and then both write to variable y. Can this pair of transactions ever cause a non-serializable execution? Justify your answer.

(iii) [3 marks] Explain what is meant by optimistic concurrency control. Under what circumstances might it perform better than pessimistic (lock-based) control?

(iv) [4 marks] Consider a transaction that reads a set of bank accounts and writes a summary total to a log file. Explain why this transaction might be susceptible to dirty reads under weak isolation levels, and describe one mechanism that could prevent such anomalies.

A distributed system uses synchronous RPC calls for inter-process communication. Process A calls Process B, which in turn calls Process C, which attempts to call Process A. Explain why the system will deadlock, and sketch a modification to the system design (not requiring changes to individual processes) that would prevent this circular wait.

Explain the difference between a semaphore and a monitor in terms of their abstraction level and the properties they guarantee. Why might a programmer prefer to use a monitor rather than semaphores for protecting a shared resource?

(i) [3 marks] Define deadlock and explain the four necessary conditions for deadlock to occur in a system of threads competing for resources.
(ii) [3 marks] What is the difference between deadlock and livelock? Can a system be both deadlocked and livelocked simultaneously?

In a distributed system with multiple processes, explain what is meant by happened-before ordering. How does Lamport's logical clock algorithm establish a total order on events, and what limitation does it have compared to vector clocks?

Consider a transaction that reads value x, performs a computation, and writes the result back to x. Explain why read-write conflicts are problematic for serialisability. Give an example of two concurrent transactions where a read-write conflict causes a non-serialisable execution.
Two grammars A and B over {a,b}. L(A) and L(B) are not equal. Give an example string in L(A) but not L(B), and an example in L(B) but not L(A).
Give regular expressions describing L(A) and L(B).
Compute nullable, first sets, and follow sets for each grammar.
State with justification whether each grammar is LL(1) and whether it is SLR(1).

You have acquired an old computer with only a Lisp compiler lispc.exe and the source code for a C compiler cc.c written in C. A useful first step is to write a quick-and-dirty C-to-Lisp compiler in Lisp. What further steps are needed to get the C compiler running efficiently? Describe and illustrate each step using the T-diagram illustration scheme.

You would like to extend the C compiler to support foreign function calls so that C programs can use libraries compiled by lispc.exe. What information from the Application Binary Interface might you need to ensure that foreign calls work correctly? Comment on function calls, on the stack, on object layout, and on any other relevant considerations.

Programs written in Lisp use a garbage collector (GC) to reclaim unreachable objects. Explain how the GC determines reachability, how calls between C and Lisp might affect the reachability check, and what changes are needed to make the check work in the presence of those calls.
Describe the inputs and outputs of a lexer and parser.
Three grammars are given for a language with branching, sequencing and variables. For each grammar, state whether it is ambiguous, giving an example if so.
For each grammar, state whether it is LL(1), giving a reason if not.
Explain the roles of the ACTION and GOTO tables in LR parsing, including what entries they contain and how they are indexed.
Two Slang simplifications: 'if true then e1 else e2 → e1' and '(fun (x:t) -> e1) e2 → {e2/x}e1'. For each, under what circumstances is it correct for type checking?
For each simplification, under what circumstances is it correct for optimisation?
The OCaml Ackermann function ack m n. Rewrite to ack_cps in continuation-passing style.
Eliminate higher-order functions: rewrite ack_cps as ack_cps_dfn (defunctionalized), using an ID constructor.
Convert ack_cps_dfn to ack_cps_dfn_list using standard lists instead of custom data types.
Comment on memory usage (stack and heap) for ack and all three transformed implementations.
Productions 'S → if E then S else S end' and 'S → if E then S end' share an initial segment. Explain why this prevents automatic LL(1) parser generation.
Rewrite those productions to be suitable for an LL(1) parser generator.
Eliminate shared initial segments from: A→aAbCeDg, A→aAbCd, B→eDgEf.
Give a general method for eliminating shared initial segments from a grammar.
Argue carefully that the grammar produced by your method generates the same language.
OCaml add_right : int list -> int. Explain why add_right as presented is not tail recursive.
CPS-transform add_right to add_right_cps : int list -> (int -> int) -> int.
Apply defunctionalisation: define data type cnt, transform to add_right_dfc : int list -> cnt -> int.
Generalised fold_right f l accu. Rewrite using CPS transformation. Justify treatment of f. What problems arise in attempting to defunctionalise?
Define what is meant by a token in lexical analysis. Explain the difference between a lexeme and a token, and give an example from a typical programming language.

For the disambiguated grammar from part (b)(ii):

(i) [2 marks] Compute the nullable, FIRST, and FOLLOW sets for all non-terminals.

(ii) [3 marks] Determine whether the grammar is LL(1). Justify your answer by checking the LL(1) conditions.

(iii) [3 marks] Determine whether the grammar is SLR(1). You need not construct the full SLR parsing table, but must justify your answer with reference to relevant parser states and conflicts.

A compiler writer is choosing between building an LL(1) recursive descent parser versus an SLR(1) table-driven parser for a language with the above grammar.

(i) [2 marks] Explain one practical advantage and one practical disadvantage of each approach.

(ii) [2 marks] If the language were extended with left-recursive productions that cannot be eliminated, which parsing strategy would be more suitable and why?
Define what is meant by a token in lexical analysis. Explain the difference between a lexeme and a token, giving a concrete example from a typical programming language.

Write regular expressions to recognise the following tokens in a simple expression language:
(i) Identifiers: sequences of letters and digits, starting with a letter
(ii) Integer literals: sequences of digits, optionally preceded by a minus sign
(iii) Operators: any of +, -, *, /, =, ==
(iv) Whitespace: spaces and tabs (which should be skipped)

Briefly explain any metacharacters or notation used in your expressions.

Describe the structure of a lexer implemented using a finite automaton. Explain how a DFA (deterministic finite automaton) can be used to recognise tokens, and what role transition tables play in the implementation. Why might an NFA be converted to a DFA for lexical analysis?

Consider the following simplified token stream produced by a lexer:

  ID LPAREN ID COMMA ID RPAREN LBRACE ID ASSIGN INT SEMICOLON RBRACE

which might represent: foo(x, y) { z = 42; }

Explain the role of the parser in processing this token stream. What does the parser construct, and what checks might it perform that the lexer cannot?

Suppose a lexer incorrectly tokenises the input string "x==y" as three tokens: ID, ASSIGN, ASSIGN, ID instead of the correct tokenisation ID, EQEQ, ID. Explain how this error would be detected by the parser, and what kind of parse error would result.
Define what is meant by a token in lexical analysis. Explain the difference between lexemes and tokens, and give a concrete example from a simple arithmetic expression language.

Consider the following grammar for simple expressions:

E → E + T | T
T → T * F | F
F → ( E ) | id

(i) [2 marks] Rewrite this grammar in a form suitable for recursive descent parsing, eliminating left recursion.

(ii) [3 marks] Sketch the structure of a recursive descent parser for your rewritten grammar, explaining how the parser would handle the input 'id + id * id'.

For the original grammar given in part (b):

(i) [3 marks] Compute the FIRST sets for all non-terminals.

(ii) [3 marks] Compute the FOLLOW sets for all non-terminals, assuming $ is the end-of-input marker.

Using your answers to part (c), determine with full justification whether the original grammar from part (b) is:

(i) [3 marks] LL(1)? If not, identify which conflict(s) arise and explain why.

(ii) [3 marks] SLR(1)? Explain your reasoning, referring to shift-reduce conflicts if applicable.

A lexer for a simple expression language uses the following token patterns:
- ID: [a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*
- NUM: [0-9]+
- PLUS: +
- TIMES: *
- LPAREN: (
- RPAREN: )

Given the input string "x123 + 42*y", show the sequence of tokens produced by the lexer, identifying any whitespace handling. Explain what would happen if the lexer attempted to tokenise "123abc" and why.

Consider the following context-free grammar G for expressions:

E → E + T | T
T → T * F | F
F → ( E ) | id

Compute the FIRST and FOLLOW sets for each non-terminal. Show your working clearly.

Using the grammar from part (b), construct the SLR(1) parse table (action and goto components). You need only show entries for terminals {+, *, (, ), id, $} and non-terminals {E, T, F}. Indicate any shift/reduce or reduce/reduce conflicts if they arise.

Trace the parsing of the string "id + id * id" using your SLR(1) parse table from part (c). Show the stack and remaining input at each step, and indicate which grammar rule is applied at each reduction.
Is the grammar in part (b) LL(1)? Justify your answer by reference to the FIRST and FOLLOW sets computed in part (b).
Explain the role of a lexical analyser in a compiler. Why is it beneficial to separate lexical analysis from syntactic analysis, rather than performing both tasks simultaneously?

Consider the following token patterns for a simple language:
  - Identifiers: [a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9]*
  - Numbers: [0-9]+
  - Operators: +, -, *, /
  - Keywords: if, then, else, while

Describe how you would construct a lexical analyser to recognise these tokens, including how you would handle the ambiguity between keywords and identifiers.

Consider the following grammar G:
  S → if E then S else S
  S → x := E
  E → E + T | T
  T → T * F | F
  F → ( E ) | id

(i) Compute the FIRST and FOLLOW sets for all non-terminals. [4 marks]
(ii) Is this grammar LL(1)? Justify your answer. [2 marks]

Consider the following ambiguous grammar:
  E → E + E | E * E | ( E ) | id

(i) Show that this grammar is ambiguous by deriving the string "id + id * id" in two different ways. [2 marks]
(ii) Explain how operator precedence and associativity can be used to disambiguate arithmetic expressions. Rewrite the grammar above to enforce standard precedence (multiplication before addition) and left-associativity. [4 marks]

A lexer uses longest-match and priority rules to resolve ambiguities. Explain what these rules mean, and give an example of how they would disambiguate between the token classes IDENT (matching [a-z][a-z0-9]*) and KEYWORD (matching 'if', 'else', 'while').

Consider an LL(1) parser for a simple expression grammar with operators + and *. Explain the role of the parse table and describe how the parser uses lookahead to decide which production rule to apply. Why does LL(1) require that the grammar satisfies certain properties?

Given the grammar:

E → T + E | T
T → F * T | F
F → ( E ) | id

Compute the FIRST and FOLLOW sets for each non-terminal. Is this grammar LL(1)? Justify your answer.

Explain the difference between LL(1) and SLR(1) parsing in terms of when parsing decisions are made and how many tokens of lookahead are used. Would the grammar in part (c) be suitable for SLR(1) parsing? Give reasons.
Define what is meant by a token in lexical analysis. Explain the difference between the lexeme and the token type, using the example of scanning the input `count = 42;`

Write regular expressions to match the following token classes in a C-like language:
(i) Identifiers: must start with a letter or underscore, followed by zero or more letters, digits, or underscores.
(ii) Floating-point constants: optional digits, a decimal point, and one or more digits; optionally followed by 'e' or 'E', an optional sign, and one or more digits.
Give your answers in standard regex notation.

Consider the following grammar for simple arithmetic expressions:

E → E + T | T
T → T * F | F
F → ( E ) | id

Compute the nullable, first, and follow sets for all non-terminals. Show your working.

Using the grammar from part (c), determine whether it is LL(1). If it is not LL(1), identify which production rule(s) cause the problem and explain why. Suggest one modification to make the grammar LL(1) if possible.
Is the grammar from part (c) suitable for shift-reduce parsing? Justify your answer with reference to shift-reduce conflicts.
Define what is meant by a token in lexical analysis. Explain the difference between a lexeme and a token, giving a concrete example from a simple programming language.

Consider the following regular expressions for tokens in a simple language:
  digit = [0-9]
  letter = [a-zA-Z]
  number = digit+
  ident = letter(letter|digit)*
  ws = [ \t\n]+

Explain why the order in which these patterns are matched by a lexer matters. Give an example of an input string where different matching orders would produce different token streams.

Consider the following context-free grammar:
  S → if E then S else S | if E then S | a
  E → b

Compute the FIRST and FOLLOW sets for all non-terminals. Is this grammar LL(1)? Justify your answer with reference to your computed sets.

Explain what is meant by shift-reduce conflict and reduce-reduce conflict in the context of LR parsing. For the grammar in part (c), determine whether it is SLR(1). If not, identify which type of conflict(s) would arise when constructing the SLR(1) parsing table.
Define what is meant by a compiler being sound with respect to a language specification. Why is soundness important in practice?
Define the collection of primitive recursive functions.
What does it mean to say that a function f: N → N is λ-definable?
For fact(x) ≜ x!, show that it is primitive recursive and explain why it is λ-definable.
For cond(x,y,z) defined as y if x=0, z otherwise: show that it is primitive recursive and explain why it is λ-definable.
Give an example of a function that is λ-definable but not primitive recursive. You do not need to give a proof.
Let P be any register machine program. What is the partial function f: N ⇀ N of one argument computed by P?
What is the partial function g: N² ⇀ N of two arguments computed by P?
Describe the construction of a Gödel numbering of register machine programs. That is, a bijection G between the natural numbers N and the collection of register machine programs.
Show that the partial function u: N² ⇀ N defined by u(i, x) = φᵢ(x) for all i and x is computable. You may assume standard results about register machine programs, as long as you state them clearly.
Sketch a proof to show that there is a computable partial function s: N² ⇀ N such that for all x, y, z ∈ N: φ_{s(x,y)}(z) = ψ_x(y, z). [S-m-n theorem]
Define configuration and computation of a register machine (RM). Explain what it means for a computation to halt.
Define RM computable partial numerical function of n arguments.
What does it mean for a problem (as a property of numbers) to be RM undecidable?

A RM computation is circular if it reaches the same configuration at two different times. Explain why circular computation doesn't halt. Give an example of a non-halting computation that is not circular.
Prove that the Circularity Problem — decide if the computation of any given RM and initial register contents is circular — is undecidable.
For the λ-calculus, define β-conversion (=_β).
Define the Church numeral n̄.
What does it mean for f: Nⁿ → N to be λ-definable? Explain why not every such function is λ-definable.
Explain why the predecessor function pred(x) = 0 if x=0, x-1 if x>0 is λ-definable and give a λ-term representing it.
Show if₀(x,y,z) = y if x=0, z if x≠0 is λ-definable.
Show and(x,y) = 0 if x=0 and y=0, 1 otherwise is λ-definable.
Show monus(x,y) = x−y if x>y, 0 if x≤y is λ-definable.
Show eq(x,y) = 0 if x=y, 1 if x≠y is λ-definable.
Define what it means for a function f: ℕ → ℕ to be primitive recursive. Give two examples of primitive recursive functions and justify your answer for each.
What does it mean to say that a function f: ℕ → ℕ is λ-definable? Give a brief intuitive explanation.

Let add(x, y) ≜ x + y be the addition function.
(i) [4 marks] Show that add is primitive recursive.
(ii) [4 marks] Explain why add is λ-definable, giving a sketch of a λ-term that defines it (you need not provide full Church numeral encodings).

Define the Halting Problem for register machines. Why does the existence of a universal register machine imply that the Halting Problem is undecidable? [Hint: consider what would happen if the Halting Problem were decidable.]

Explain briefly what a universal register machine is and describe the relationship between the Church-Turing thesis and the equivalence of universal register machines, Turing machines, and λ-definable functions.
Define what is meant by a register machine and explain the purpose of each of the three types of instruction in the standard model.
What is a universal register machine? Why is the existence of such a machine significant for computability theory?
State the Halting Problem precisely. Explain why the Halting Problem is undecidable, making reference to a proof technique.
Explain the Church-Turing thesis. Discuss how the equivalence between Turing machines and register machines provides evidence for this thesis.

Define the class of partially recursive functions. Explain how the partially recursive functions relate to functions computable by register machines, and give an example of a partially recursive function that is not total.
Define a register machine and explain what it means for a function to be computable by a register machine.
Define the collection of primitive recursive functions, and give two examples of primitive recursive functions with brief justification.
What does it mean to say that a function f: ℕ → ℕ is λ-definable? Explain briefly why the successor function succ(n) = n + 1 is λ-definable.
Consider the function add(x, y) = x + y. Show that add is primitive recursive by expressing it in terms of the successor function and primitive recursion.
State the Halting Problem. Explain why the Halting Problem is undecidable, and discuss the relationship between this result and the existence of universal register machines.
Define a register machine, explaining what is meant by the instruction set and the notion of halting.
What is a universal register machine? Explain briefly why the existence of such a machine is significant for computability theory.
State the Halting Problem. Explain what it means to say that the Halting Problem is undecidable, and outline why no register machine can solve it.
Compare the computational power of Turing machines and register machines. Do they compute the same class of functions? Justify your answer.
Define what it means for a function f: ℕ → ℕ to be Turing-computable. Give an example of a total function that is Turing-computable but not primitive recursive.
Define the collection of primitive recursive functions, including the base cases and closure properties.
What does it mean to say that a function f: ℕ → ℕ is λ-definable? Give a brief example of a simple λ-definable function.

Consider the function pred(x) defined as x−1 if x > 0, and 0 if x = 0 (the predecessor function). Show that pred is primitive recursive by expressing it in terms of primitive recursive operations, and explain why it must be λ-definable.

Consider the function min(x,y) which returns the minimum of x and y. Is min primitive recursive? Justify your answer by explaining how min can be constructed from simpler primitive recursive functions.
Give an example of a total computable function that is λ-definable but not primitive recursive. You do not need to provide a full proof, but briefly explain why such a function must exist.
Define a register machine and explain what is meant by a program for a register machine. What is the significance of the instruction set?
What is a universal register machine? Explain briefly why the existence of a universal register machine is important for computability theory.

Consider a register machine program P that computes f(x) = 2x. (i) [3 marks] Write out an explicit program for P using the standard instruction set (INC, DEC, JUMP). (ii) [4 marks] Explain how one might use a universal register machine to determine whether an arbitrary program Q halts on input n. Why does this lead to a contradiction for certain choices of Q and n?
State the Halting Problem formally. Prove by contradiction that there is no register machine program that solves the Halting Problem.
Compare and contrast the computational power of Turing machines and register machines. Are there any functions computable by one but not the other?
Define the collection of primitive recursive functions, making clear the base cases and closure properties.
Explain what it means for a total function f: ℕ → ℕ to be λ-definable. What is the significance of Church's thesis in this context?
Let add(x,y) denote x + y. Show that add is primitive recursive, and explain why it must be λ-definable.

Let pred(x) be the predecessor function, defined as x−1 if x > 0 and 0 if x = 0. Show that pred is primitive recursive by giving an explicit definition in terms of primitive recursive operations. Hence argue that pred is λ-definable.
Give an example of a total function that is λ-definable but not primitive recursive. You do not need to prove your answer.
Compare the expressive power of primitive recursive functions with that of functions computable by register machines. What additional computational capability do register machines possess?
Define a register machine and explain what is meant by a program for a register machine. What does it mean for a register machine to halt?
Explain what is meant by a universal register machine. Why is the existence of a universal register machine significant for the theory of computation?
State the Halting Problem. Explain why there is no computable function that can solve the Halting Problem for arbitrary programs and inputs.
Define a Turing machine formally, including the definition of its configuration and the notion of a computation step. What does it mean for a Turing machine to accept a language?
Explain the difference between a total recursive function and a partial recursive function. Give an example of a partial recursive function that is not total recursive, and justify your answer.
Provide a precise definition of the following complexity classes: EXP, NEXP, and NPSPACE.
Prove that NPSPACE ⊆ EXP ⊆ NEXP.
Prove that if P = NP, then EXP = NEXP.
What is a Hamiltonian cycle in a graph G=(V,E)?
What is known about the complexity of deciding whether a graph has a Hamiltonian cycle?
Show that G has a Hamiltonian cycle not including edge e if and only if G−e has a Hamiltonian cycle.
Assume P=NP. Show there is a polynomial-time algorithm A that returns 'no' if G has no Hamiltonian cycle, or returns an actual Hamiltonian cycle otherwise.
How would you modify the definition of pseudo one-way function to obtain Papadimitriou's one-way function definition?
Show that for any pseudo one-way function f, the language Lf is in NP.
Show that for any pseudo one-way function f, the language Lf is not in P.
Given function s(x) defined using a SAT formula. Prove that if P≠NP then s is a pseudo one-way function.
Give a precise definition of a logarithmic-space reduction A ≤_L B.
Show that A ≤_L B and B ≤_L C implies A ≤_L C.
For each of P, NP, NL, co-NP, give an example of a problem complete for that class under log-space reductions.
For each pair of problems from (c), state whether A ≤_L B, or if unknown, state what complexity class inclusions would follow.
A graph homomorphism is a mapping G→H preserving edges. HOM = set of pairs (G,H) with a homomorphism. Explain why HOM is in NP.
Let K₃ have vertices a,b,c with edges {a,b},{b,c},{a,c}. Show that there is a homomorphism from G to K₃ if and only if G is 3-colourable.
What can you conclude about the complexity of HOM?
Let K₂ have vertices a,b with edge {a,b}. What is the complexity of deciding if there is a homomorphism from G to K₂?
Define the complexity classes P and NP precisely. In your definition of NP, explain the role of the verification algorithm and the certificate.

The 3-SAT problem is NP-complete. Explain what this means, and outline why the satisfiability problem SAT can be reduced to 3-SAT in polynomial time. You need not provide the full technical details of the reduction, but should explain the key idea.
Define the complexity class coNP and explain the relationship between coNP and NP. Is it known whether NP = coNP? Justify your answer.
Give an example of a problem in P and briefly explain why it is in P (i.e., state a polynomial-time algorithm for it, without a full proof of correctness).
Define precisely what it means for a decision problem to be in NP. Your definition should make clear the role of the certificate (or witness) and the verification algorithm.

The 3-SAT problem asks whether a given Boolean formula in conjunctive normal form with clauses of size at most 3 is satisfiable. Explain why 3-SAT is NP-complete. In your answer, you should: (i) explain why 3-SAT is in NP; (ii) explain the significance of the Cook-Levin theorem in establishing NP-completeness; (iii) discuss briefly how one would reduce SAT to 3-SAT if needed.

Define the complexity class coNP and explain the relationship between NP and coNP. Is it known whether NP = coNP? What would be the consequences for complexity theory if P = NP ∩ coNP? Justify your answer.
Define the complexity classes P and NP. In your definition, make clear the role of the verifier in characterising NP.
The 3-SAT problem is NP-complete. Explain what this means, and briefly describe how the Cook-Levin theorem establishes NP-completeness of 3-SAT.
Prove that if a language L is in NP, then its complement L̄ is in coNP. Is it known whether NP = coNP? Justify your answer.
Explain the relationship between the P versus NP problem and the existence of efficient algorithms for NP-complete problems. What would be the consequences if P = NP?
Define the complexity classes NP and coNP precisely. Explain the significance of the relationship between these classes.

Compare NP-completeness and coNP-completeness. If a problem L is NP-complete, what can you deduce about the complexity of its complement L̄? Justify your answer with reference to the definitions of NP and coNP.
Prove that if P = NP, then NP = coNP. State the contrapositive of this result and explain its significance for the P versus NP question.
Define the complexity classes P and NP precisely. In your definition, explain the role of a polynomial-time verifier for NP.
Define what it means for a problem to be NP-complete. Show that if any NP-complete problem is in P, then P = NP.
The 3-SAT problem is known to be NP-complete. Explain briefly why this implies that 3-SAT ∈ P if and only if P = NP.
Define the complexity class coNP. Explain what is meant by saying that a problem is coNP-complete, and give an example of a coNP-complete problem.
Define the complexity classes NP and coNP. Explain what it means for a language to be NP-complete, and state the Cook-Levin theorem.

The satisfiability problem SAT is NP-complete. By carefully constructing a polynomial-time reduction, prove that the 3-SAT problem (satisfiability of formulas in conjunctive normal form with exactly 3 literals per clause) is also NP-complete. You may assume SAT is NP-complete.

Compare the complexity classes NP and coNP. Discuss what it would mean if NP = coNP, and explain why this is considered unlikely. In your answer, consider the implications for the structure of complexity classes and give an example of a problem in coNP that is not known to be in NP.
Define the complexity classes P and NP. In your definition, explain what is meant by a 'polynomial-time verifier' and how it relates to the definition of NP.

The 3-SAT problem is NP-complete. Explain what this means, and describe briefly how one would prove that a new decision problem Π is NP-complete using 3-SAT. Give an example of another NP-complete problem and state (without proof) why it is NP-complete.
Define the class coNP. Explain why it is widely believed (but unproven) that NP ≠ coNP. What would be the consequence if P = NP for the relationship between P and coNP?
Show that the class P is closed under polynomial-time reductions. That is, prove: if A ∈ P and B reduces to A in polynomial time, then B ∈ P.
Define the complexity classes NP and coNP precisely. Explain what it means for a language to be in coNP in terms of verifiable certificates.
Prove that if P = NP, then P = coNP. Explain why this would imply that every language in NP has a polynomial-time verifiable certificate for both membership and non-membership.

The language UNSAT is defined as the set of Boolean formulas that are unsatisfiable. Explain why UNSAT is coNP-complete. Is UNSAT known to be in NP? Justify your answer by discussing the relationship between NP and coNP.
A two-class linear classifier uses Pr(C₁|x,w,w₀,θ) = σ_θ(wᵀx + w₀) where σ_θ(x) = 1/(1+exp(−θx)). What role does θ play when set freely?

Data has a band-like structure (C₂, C₁, C₂ bands). How can the linear classifier be modified — changing only its activation function with no new parameters — to be trained on this data with a trainable band width?
Using cross-entropy error E(p) = −Σ[yᵢ log h(xᵢ,p) + (1−yᵢ) log(1−h(xᵢ,p))], explain how gradient descent applies to the modified classifier in (b), giving explicit gradient formulae.
In Partial-Order Planning (POP), how does an ordering constraint differ from a causal link?
Why must Partial-Order Planning consider both ordering constraints and causal links?
A tile-placement problem: explain how Start and Finish states are represented in POP.
Explain how actions modelling tile placement are represented in POP, with examples from the tile-placement instance.
Explain promotion and demotion in POP. To what extent are they useful in this planning problem given your formulation? Illustrate with an example.
In a state-variable planning representation for an Evil Robot maze, give an example of a domain.
Give an example of a rigid relation in the representation.
Give an example of a function/state variable representing the destination of a switched edge.
Give an example of a goal in the representation.
Explain in detail how actions for (1) moving around the grid and (2) altering a switched edge's destination are implemented in the state-variable representation.
The victim converts the planning problem to a CSP. Explain in detail, with examples from the diagram, the steps for this conversion.
K-class classification using softmax: Pr(x ∈ class i|x,w) = exp(h(wᵢ,x)) / Σ exp(h(wⱼ,x)). Explain why the softmax modification is required and how it achieves its aim.
Maximise Pr(s|w). Show training can be achieved by minimising E(w) = −Σᵢ Σⱼ yᵢⱼ log prob(j, xᵢ). State all assumptions made.
Given ∂h(wᵢ,x)/∂wᵢⱼ from backpropagation, explain what further steps are needed to obtain the derivatives of E(w) with respect to relevant weights.
GraphPlan planning graph. Draw state levels S_i and action levels A_i up to S₂ (no mutexes yet).
Explain why a plan cannot be extracted from the graph at this stage.
What is the smallest i for which plan extraction might be attempted from S_i? Explain.
Draw levels S_{i-1}, A_{i-1}, S_i for that i.
Mark four mutexes, each from a different reason. Explain each type of mutex.
Describe generally how plan extraction from a planning graph can be addressed as a heuristic search problem.
Variation on MLP with p groups of n inputs and shared weight vector w. Show that δ = ∂E(θ)/∂a = σ'(a) · ∂E(θ)/∂y.
Find expressions for partial derivatives of E(θ) with respect to parameters of the output node.
Show that δᵢ = ∂E(θ)/∂aᵢ = δ vᵢ σ'(aᵢ).
Find expressions for partial derivatives of E(θ) with respect to parameters of the hidden nodes.
Define what is meant by an uninformed search strategy. Give two examples of uninformed search algorithms and explain why they are considered 'uninformed'.

Consider a search problem on a graph where the goal is to find the shortest path from a start node s to a goal node g. Depth-first search (DFS) explores nodes in depth-first order, while breadth-first search (BFS) explores nodes level by level. (i) Explain why BFS is guaranteed to find the shortest path in an unweighted graph, but DFS is not. (ii) What is the time and space complexity of BFS in terms of the branching factor b and depth d? (iii) Describe one practical limitation of BFS when applied to real-world problems with large branching factors.

Greedy best-first search uses a heuristic function h(n) to estimate the cost from node n to the goal. A* search uses f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where g(n) is the actual cost from the start node to n. (i) Explain what is meant by an admissible heuristic and why this property is important for A*. (ii) Show that if h₁ and h₂ are both admissible heuristics, then h(n) = max(h₁(n), h₂(n)) is also admissible. (iii) Compare the completeness and optimality guarantees of A* versus greedy best-first search. Under what conditions might greedy best-first search be preferable despite these guarantees?
Define what is meant by an uninformed search strategy in the context of state-space search. Give two examples of uninformed search algorithms and explain why they are considered 'uninformed'.

Consider a maze problem where an agent must find a path from a start state S to a goal state G. Compare the performance of breadth-first search (BFS) and depth-first search (DFS) on this problem in terms of: (i) completeness, (ii) optimality, and (iii) space complexity. Under what circumstances might DFS be preferable to BFS despite its drawbacks?

Explain the concept of an admissible heuristic and why it is important for ensuring optimal solutions in A* search. Consider a heuristic h(n) for the maze problem that estimates the straight-line distance from node n to the goal. Is this heuristic always admissible? Justify your answer with an example scenario where it may or may not be admissible.

Define the term 'search space' in the context of uninformed search algorithms. Explain why the branching factor and depth of a search tree are critical factors when analysing the time and space complexity of breadth-first search (BFS).

Compare depth-first search (DFS) and breadth-first search (BFS) with respect to: (i) completeness, (ii) optimality of the solution found, and (iii) memory requirements. In your answer, explain what conditions on the search problem determine when each algorithm is preferable to the other.

Consider the A* search algorithm with evaluation function f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where g(n) is the cost from the start node and h(n) is a heuristic estimate of cost to the goal. Explain what it means for a heuristic to be admissible, and prove that if h(n) is admissible, then A* will find an optimal path to the goal. Why is the consistency (monotonicity) condition h(n) ≤ c(n,n') + h(n') a stronger requirement than admissibility?
Define breadth-first search (BFS) and explain why it is guaranteed to find a solution if one exists. What is the time complexity of BFS in terms of the branching factor b and solution depth d?

Consider the A* algorithm with evaluation function f(n) = g(n) + h(n). Explain what properties a heuristic h(n) must satisfy to be admissible. Show why an admissible heuristic guarantees that A* finds an optimal solution. Would the algorithm still find an optimal solution if h(n) sometimes overestimates the true cost to the goal? Justify your answer.

A search problem requires finding a path from state S to state G in a graph with 1000 nodes. Two heuristics are available: h₁ which is admissible with average error of 5% of true cost, and h₂ which is also admissible but has average error of 15%. (i) Which heuristic would you expect A* to expand fewer nodes with, and why? (ii) Suppose h₂ dominates h₁ (i.e. h₂(n) ≥ h₁(n) for all n). How does this affect your answer? (iii) Would uniform cost search (Dijkstra's algorithm) be more or less efficient than A* with h₁ on this problem?
Define what is meant by an uninformed search strategy. Give two examples of uninformed search algorithms and explain briefly why they are considered 'uninformed'.

Consider a graph search problem where the branching factor is b and the solution depth is d. Compare breadth-first search (BFS) and depth-first search (DFS) in terms of:
(i) Time complexity
(ii) Space complexity
(iii) Completeness and optimality
Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other?

A* search uses the evaluation function f(n) = g(n) + h(n), where g(n) is the cost from the initial state to node n, and h(n) is a heuristic estimate of the cost from n to a goal state.
(i) Explain what properties a heuristic h(n) must satisfy for A* to guarantee finding an optimal solution. [4 marks]
(ii) Consider two admissible heuristics h₁ and h₂ for a search problem. Define what it means for h₂ to dominate h₁, and explain why a dominant heuristic would be preferable in A* search. [3 marks]
(iii) If h(n) = 0 for all nodes, what search algorithm does A* reduce to, and what are the implications for solution quality? [3 marks]
Define breadth-first search (BFS) and explain why it is guaranteed to find a solution if one exists. What is the primary disadvantage of BFS in terms of space complexity?

Consider the A* algorithm with evaluation function f(n) = g(n) + h(n). Explain what conditions a heuristic h(n) must satisfy to be admissible. If h(n) is admissible, prove that A* will find an optimal solution. Why might an admissible heuristic still lead to high computational cost in practice?

You are designing a pathfinding system for a robot navigating a grid-based maze. The robot can move up, down, left, or right (4-connected movement), and each move has unit cost. Compare the use of BFS versus A* with Manhattan distance heuristic for this problem. Give explicit time and space complexity bounds for both approaches when the grid is n×n with k obstacles. Under what circumstances would you prefer BFS over A*, if any?

Suppose you have two admissible heuristics h₁(n) and h₂(n) for the same problem. Define what it means for h₁ to dominate h₂. Explain how you could construct a new heuristic h₃(n) = max(h₁(n), h₂(n)) and prove that it remains admissible.
Define what is meant by a search problem in AI. What are the key components that must be specified when formalising a search problem, and why is the explicit definition of a goal test important?

Compare breadth-first search (BFS) and depth-first search (DFS) in terms of: (i) completeness, (ii) optimality of solutions found, (iii) space complexity, and (iv) time complexity. For each comparison, briefly explain the reasoning. Under what circumstances would you prefer DFS over BFS?

Consider the A* search algorithm using evaluation function f(n) = g(n) + h(n). (i) Define what is meant by an admissible heuristic and explain why A* is guaranteed to find an optimal solution when h(n) is admissible. (ii) A heuristic h₁ dominates h₂ if h₁(n) ≥ h₂(n) for all nodes n and both are admissible. Why would a programmer prefer to use the dominating heuristic h₁ in A* search? (iii) Give a concrete example of an admissible heuristic for the 8-puzzle problem and explain why it is admissible.
Define what is meant by an uninformed search strategy. Give two examples of uninformed search algorithms and explain why they are considered 'uninformed'.

Consider a graph-based pathfinding problem where an agent must navigate from a start node S to a goal node G. Breadth-First Search (BFS) is applied to this graph.
(i) Explain why BFS will always find the shortest path in terms of number of edges. [3 marks]
(ii) A heuristic function h(n) is now introduced, estimating the cost from node n to the goal. What property must h(n) satisfy to guarantee that A* search finds an optimal path? Define this property formally. [4 marks]

In a two-player zero-sum game, the Minimax algorithm with alpha-beta pruning is used to search a game tree to a fixed depth d.
(i) Explain how alpha-beta pruning reduces the number of nodes evaluated compared to standard Minimax, providing a worked example with a small game tree (at least 3 levels) where at least one branch is pruned. [6 marks]
(ii) Compare the best-case and worst-case time complexity of alpha-beta pruning with standard Minimax search. Under what conditions does alpha-beta pruning achieve its best-case complexity? [4 marks]
Are C functions exactly the same as C++ functions? What is the difference between a C function and a C++ method?
A system where char is 8–16 bits but int is always 24 bits. Write a program to determine the number of bits in a char.
char is 8 bits but signedness is unknown. Write a program to test whether char is signed or unsigned.
Can C++ upcasts and downcasts always be checked for type safety at compile time?
Give an example of a C++ upcast that requires the object pointer to be adjusted.
Give an example where a C++ downcast is checked at runtime. What is needed for the language runtime to check validity?
Will 'char *mys = "ab_de"; mys[2] = 'c';' work? Why or why not?
A C programmer sorts an array of pointers to structs using <. Describe one circumstance producing undefined behaviour and one where behaviour is defined.

Criticise a given bucket<T> template class definition. Give three reasons to separate declaration and implementation in OO. Explain difficulties with implementation in a separate file. Does inheriting from a common parent help?
Instead of holding pointers, a collection holds objects themselves. What method type changes are needed? Advantages and disadvantages?
All pushed objects must have void foo(). bucket invokes foo() on all members on removal. Define storage and sketch dequeue(). Can foo() use static or dynamic dispatch?

14-bit words: 5-bit unsigned mantissa, 9-bit unsigned left-shift exponent. Write two C functions: one to convert to nearest 32-bit unsigned int and one to nearest double-precision float. What problems arise?

A packed array of 14-bit words with no unused bits (words may cross byte boundaries). Write a C function to update a word at a given index. Args: unsigned char* base pointer, index, 14-bit word to store. May assume unaligned 32-bit loads/stores supported.
Implement void *my_malloc(size_t) that calls system malloc but adds 16 bytes padding at start and end, initialised with a distinctive bit pattern.
Write companion my_free that checks the padding pattern for changes, reports appropriately, or calls system free.
What is the motivation for introducing my_malloc and my_free?
Describe the two main forms of dynamic storage in C and OCaml. How does each interact with a garbage collector?
Does any aspect of C or C++ prevent the compiler from implementing tail-recursion optimisation? Would this tend to reduce memory leak problems?

In-core database: server process and 4 client processes share a memory segment. Describe the total number of user-space segments in physical memory. How might memory in the shared segment be allocated?
C++ multiple inheritance: class D: B,C { int d1, d2; }. Give C code summarising the storage layout of D x.
Given class D *p, how are C++-style casts (B*)p and (C*)p achieved in C code?
If B and C both inherit from A, explain the conceptual choice for how A's data members appear in D's layout.
Explain how the virtual keyword lets the programmer select between the alternatives in (iii).
Explain the key differences between C and C++ function declarations. In particular, discuss how C++ function overloading differs from C's approach to functions with the same logical purpose.
Write a C program that determines the exact number of bytes occupied by a pointer on the target system. Your program should print the result in a portable way without using sizeof.

Consider the declaration 'int *p = NULL; int x = *p;'. Explain why this code is undefined behaviour in C, and discuss what actually happens on a typical system. Is it possible for a compiler to detect this error at compile time?

In C++, when you dynamically allocate an object with 'new', explain the role of the vtable in supporting runtime type information (RTTI). Give an example of a class hierarchy where the vtable is essential for correct behaviour.

Compare C-style casts (e.g., '(int *)p') with C++ style casts (e.g., 'static_cast<int*>(p)'). Why did C++ introduce multiple cast types, and which situations require dynamic_cast instead of static_cast?

Describe what a memory leak is and give a concrete example in C++. Explain how RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization) can be used to prevent memory leaks, and provide a brief code example using std::unique_ptr.
Will the code 'char *s = "hello"; s[0] = 'H';' work correctly? Justify your answer by explaining the storage location of string literals in C.

Explain the difference between C-style casts and C++ casts. In particular, describe what static_cast, dynamic_cast, const_cast, and reinterpret_cast are used for, and give an example where each would be appropriate.

In C++, explain what is meant by "pointer-to-member" syntax (the .* and ->* operators). Write a short code example showing how you might use these operators, and explain a situation where they are more useful than virtual functions.

A program uses new to allocate memory but accidentally calls free() instead of delete to deallocate it. Explain why this is problematic and what runtime errors or undefined behaviour might result. Would a memory debugging tool like valgrind necessarily catch this error?

Explain what spatial and temporal locality mean in the context of CPU caches. Give an example of C code that exhibits poor cache locality, and rewrite it to improve locality. How might this rewrite affect program performance?

Explain the difference between a C function declaration and a C++ function declaration. In particular, discuss how C++ name mangling differs from C, and explain why this difference exists when linking object files.
Write a C program that determines at runtime whether your system allocates 4 or 8 bytes for a pointer. Your program should output the result clearly and must not rely on sizeof().

Describe three different dynamic memory errors that can occur in C programs (e.g., double-free, use-after-free, buffer overflow). For each error, explain a simple runtime or compile-time checking technique that could detect it.

Compare C-style casts with C++ static_cast, dynamic_cast, and reinterpret_cast. Explain when each should be used, and give a concrete example where using reinterpret_cast could be unsafe but dynamic_cast would be safer.

Explain how cache locality affects the performance of array access in C. Write a simple program that demonstrates the difference between row-major and column-major traversal of a 2D array, and explain why one is faster than the other.

Define what is meant by a function signature in C. Explain how C++ function overloading allows multiple functions with the same name but different signatures to coexist, and why this is not possible in C.
In C++, explain the difference between a pointer and a reference. Can a reference be reassigned to refer to a different object after initialization? Illustrate your answer with a short code example.

In C++, explain the purpose of RTTI (Run-Time Type Information) and the role of dynamic_cast. When might dynamic_cast return a null pointer? Give an example involving class inheritance where dynamic_cast would be necessary to safely downcast a pointer.

Discuss the memory layout of a simple class in C++ with one virtual method and two member variables (an int and a double). Explain where the virtual method table (vtable) pointer is stored and how this affects memory usage and performance compared to a class without virtual methods.

Define a buffer overflow and explain why it is a critical security vulnerability in C programs. Give an example of how strcpy() can lead to a buffer overflow, and name one safer alternative function that should be used instead.
Explain the key differences between C functions and C++ methods. In particular, discuss how name mangling affects function calls and why C++ requires name mangling whilst C does not.
On a system where the size of int is unknown, write a C program that determines the exact number of bits in an int without using sizeof().
The signedness of char is implementation-defined. Write a C program to determine whether char is signed or unsigned on your platform, without relying on compiler documentation.

Consider the declaration: int *p = malloc(sizeof(int)); free(p); int x = *p; Explain why this code exhibits undefined behaviour and what might happen at runtime. How could a debugging tool detect this error?

In C++, explain the difference between static_cast and dynamic_cast when used with pointers to derived classes. Give an example where dynamic_cast succeeds but static_cast would be unsafe, and explain what runtime information is required for dynamic_cast to work correctly.
Explain what is meant by spatial and temporal locality in the context of the memory hierarchy. Give an example of a C program with poor cache locality and suggest how it could be optimised.
Define what is meant by the term 'name mangling' in C++. Why is name mangling necessary in C++ but not in C? Give an example of how a function name might be mangled.
Write a C program fragment that demonstrates the difference between a stack-allocated array and a heap-allocated array. Include proper memory management and explain the lifetime of each.

In C++, explain the difference between `static_cast`, `dynamic_cast`, and `reinterpret_cast`. For which cast would you expect runtime overhead, and why? Give an example where `reinterpret_cast` might be unsafe.

Consider pointer arithmetic: `char *s = "hello"; char c = *(s + 2);`. Explain what value `c` contains and why this is safe. Now consider `int *p = (int *)s; int x = *(p + 1);`. Is this safe? Justify your answer with reference to alignment and memory layout.
Explain the concept of memory fragmentation in C. How might using a custom memory allocator help reduce external fragmentation? Give one trade-off of implementing a custom allocator.
Explain the difference between a C function declaration and a C++ function declaration. What is name mangling and why is it necessary in C++ but not in C?
Write a C program that uses pointer arithmetic to determine the size of a struct containing a char, an int, and a double. Explain what alignment and padding are, and why they affect the result.
Write a C program that deliberately causes a buffer overflow on the stack. Explain how a debugger could help detect this issue, and what sanitizing tools (such as AddressSanitizer) could catch.
Compare C-style casts with C++ style casts (static_cast, dynamic_cast, const_cast, reinterpret_cast). Give an example where using const_cast is necessary and explain the risks involved.
A C++ program repeatedly allocates and deallocates small objects in a loop. Explain how memory fragmentation could occur and suggest two strategies to mitigate this problem.
Define what is meant by undefined behaviour in C. Give two examples of C code that exhibit undefined behaviour and explain why each is problematic.
Explain the difference between C-style casts and C++ static_cast. When would you use each? Give a concrete example where using a C-style cast could mask a type error that static_cast would prevent.

Consider a C++ class hierarchy where Base is a parent class and Derived is a child class. Explain when a dynamic_cast from Base* to Derived* will return NULL at runtime. What RTTI mechanism enables this check? Why is this safer than a C-style cast in this scenario?

Explain the term 'cache locality'. How does the layout of data in memory affect cache performance? Give an example of a C data structure that exhibits poor cache locality and suggest how it could be improved.
Define eval/2 to reduce arithmetic terms: eval(1+2*3, N) gives N=7, atoms reduce to themselves.
Extend eval to support function calls: 2 * apply(inc, [2+3]) reduces to 12, where fun(apply(inc,[N]), N+1) is a fact. Function args in a list.
Extend eval to support the ==/2 operator: A==B reduces to true if A and B reduce to the same number or atom, false otherwise.
Extend eval to support if(Condition, Then, Else). Add a fun fact for factorial so eval(apply(fact,[5]), N) gives N=120.
Explain Prolog's unification process. What does an occurs check guard against?
node(Left,Right) and leaf(Name,Value) trees. Define lookup(+Tree, ?Name, ?Value).
Define rle(+L1, ?L2) to run-length encode a list: rle([a,a,b,c,a,a,a], L) gives L=[2*a, 1*b, 1*c, 3*a]. Would rle(L, [2*a, 1*b, 1*c, 3*a]) succeed in reverse? Give reasons.
Define rld(+L1, ?L2) to decode a run-length-encoded list.

Given alter_list and change clauses, add a difference-list argument to accumulate the execution path through Prolog clauses as a list of clause numbers. alter_list([2,4,6], L, Path-[]) should yield L=[a,a,b] and Path=[4,1,4,1,4,2,3].

Define a predicate append/3 that concatenates two lists. Your definition should work in all directions: append([1,2], [3,4], L) should give L=[1,2,3,4], and append(X, Y, [a,b,c]) should generate all possible splits. Give two example queries and their results.

Define a predicate member/2 that checks membership in a list using backtracking. Write a query that uses member to find all elements greater than 2 in the list [1,3,2,4,1]. Explain how backtracking allows member to generate multiple solutions.

Define a predicate path/3 that finds a path between two nodes in a directed graph represented as edges. The graph is given by facts of the form edge(X, Y). Your predicate should avoid infinite loops by tracking visited nodes. Write a fact-based representation of a small graph with at least 4 nodes and give an example query.

Explain the concept of difference lists and how they improve efficiency in list operations. Define a predicate append_dl/3 for difference lists that appends two difference lists in constant time. Give an example showing how append_dl([1,2|X]-X, [3,4|Y]-Y, R) is converted back to ordinary list form.

Define a predicate eval/2 that evaluates arithmetic expressions containing +, -, *, and / operators. For example, eval(2+3*4, N) should unify N with 14. Atoms (that are not operators) should evaluate to themselves.

Extend eval/2 to support predicate calls of the form call(Functor, Args), where Args is a list of arguments. For example, given the fact rule(succ, [N], N+1), the query eval(call(succ, [5]), X) should unify X with 6. Assume that rules are stored as facts of the form rule(Functor, ArgList, Body).

Extend eval/2 to handle comparison operators =:= and \=. The expression (3+2) =:= (1+4) should evaluate to true, and (2*3) \= 5 should evaluate to true. Non-numeric atoms should cause the comparison to fail gracefully.

Extend eval/2 to support conditional expressions of the form ite(Condition, ThenExpr, ElseExpr), which evaluates Condition and executes either ThenExpr or ElseExpr accordingly. Give an example showing how to define a recursive factorial using ite and call/2.

Define a predicate append/3 that concatenates two lists. Your definition should work in all modes: append([1,2], [3,4], L) gives L=[1,2,3,4], and append(X, Y, [a,b,c]) should generate all possible splits on backtracking.

Define a predicate member/2 that succeeds if its first argument is a member of the list given as its second argument. Explain how backtracking allows member(X, [1,2,3]) to generate X=1, X=2, X=3 on successive calls to redo. Give the definition.

Define a predicate length/2 that relates a list to its length. Your definition should allow both length([a,b,c], N) to compute N=3, and length(L, 2) to generate lists of length 2 on backtracking. How does your definition handle the case length(L, N) where both are unbound?

Define a predicate last/2 that relates a list to its last element. For example, last([1,2,3], X) gives X=3. Your solution should be efficient and work correctly when the list is given but the last element is unbound. Compare your approach to a naive implementation that uses append/3.

Define a predicate member/2 that succeeds if its first argument is a member of the list given as its second argument. For example, member(2, [1,2,3]) should succeed and member(4, [1,2,3]) should fail. Your definition should work in both directions: member(X, [1,2,3]) should generate X=1, X=2, X=3 on backtracking.

Define a predicate append/3 such that append(L1, L2, L3) succeeds if L3 is the concatenation of lists L1 and L2. For example, append([1,2], [3,4], L) gives L=[1,2,3,4], and append(X, Y, [a,b,c]) should generate all possible splits on backtracking. Show how your definition handles the case where all three arguments are uninstantiated.

Define a predicate length/2 such that length(L, N) relates a list L to its length N. Your definition should support both directions: length([1,2,3], N) should give N=3, and length(L, 2) should generate lists of length 2. Explain why a naive recursive definition might be inefficient for generating lists of a given length.

Define a predicate select/3 such that select(X, L, R) succeeds if X is an element of list L and R is L with X removed. For example, select(2, [1,2,3], [1,3]) should succeed. Your definition should allow generating all possible selections through backtracking. How does select/3 differ from member/2 in terms of what information it provides?
Precomputed hash chains for cracking lowercase alphanumeric passwords (≤15 chars). Derive formulae and numerical values for the maximum number of chains n_c and minimum chain length l_c.
Describe the algorithm for recovering a password that is the preimage of a given digest d using the hash chain table.
Derive the formula and numerical value for the minimum number of hashes n_h needed to build the table.
Calculate the time t_s to build the table sequentially. Give speedup factors for CPU→GPU, a bank of GPUs, and distributed collaborators. Compute total time with all three speedups.
Explain what hash collisions are and why they matter. How would collisions invalidate the answer to (a)(iii)?
Buffer overflow hacking game. 32-bit x86, setuid challenge program, executable stack, no canaries, ASLR off. Do you favour more runs or longer inputs? Explain.
Should you put the nop sled/payload before or after the stack spraying region? Explain.
Do you favour a longer nop sled or a longer stack spraying region?
Outline an efficient strategy guaranteeing a win, including the worst-case number of runs.
Give specific values of each of the five input parameters (length, payloadOffset, retOffset, retAddress, retCopies) for run i.

A Python program reads fields.txt (two text fields), concatenates with LaTeX template fragments, writes to form-content.tex, then runs pdflatex via os.system(). Can a fields.txt cause malfunction during lines 1–8? If yes, produce such input; otherwise justify.
Can a fields.txt cause malfunction during the os.system() call? If yes, produce such input; otherwise justify.
Amend the program so it won't malfunction on inputs from (a) or (b). An ad-hoc fix is sufficient.
Can an attacker exploit this to execute an arbitrary command? Under what circumstances? If yes, produce input to execute /tmp/payload.
For security training: state the two most important points and recommend remedial approaches, in the context of the listing.
Compare a mechanical master-keyed lock system vs electronic key fobs under at least four aspects. Give one scenario for each where that system is preferable, with justification.

Building with 24 locks D0–D23, 5 pin stacks, 8 cut depths, at most one master pin per stack. D10–D19 opened by master key 36735. For D12, what is the minimum and maximum number of pin stacks containing a master pin?
D13 differ key is 32737. List all other keys that also open D13, with stated assumptions.

D16 occupant has differ key 32535 and wants to open D07 using Matt Blaze privilege escalation, visiting D07 only once. Using optimal strategy, list every key created and tried in order, with lock and result.

Define a salt in the context of password storage. Explain why salts are essential for preventing rainbow table attacks, and describe what information an attacker would need to construct an effective rainbow table for salted passwords.

Consider a password authentication system using bcrypt with a cost parameter of 12. Derive the approximate computational time required to verify a single password on a modern CPU (assume SHA-256 as baseline: ~1 ns per operation). How would this change if an attacker possessed a GPU capable of 10^12 operations per second? Give numerical estimates for both cases.

Explain the difference between dictionary attacks and brute-force attacks on password hashes. For a lowercase alphanumeric password of length 8, calculate the keyspace size and estimate the average number of attempts required for each attack method.

Compare the security properties of passwords, passphrases, and passkeys (public-key cryptography-based authentication). Discuss the trade-offs between security and usability for each method. Why have organisations begun mandating passkeys for sensitive accounts?
Define what is meant by a credential stuffing attack and explain why it poses a threat even to users with strong, unique passwords.
Describe three technical countermeasures against credential stuffing attacks. For each, explain its effectiveness and any limitations.

Discuss why users frequently choose weak passwords despite security awareness training. Suggest two practical mechanisms that organisations could implement to encourage stronger password practices without significantly degrading user experience.
Define authentication and explain the difference between identification and authentication. Give an example of a system where these two concepts are conflated, and explain the security implications.

Describe the challenge-response authentication protocol. Explain why it is preferable to password-based authentication for remote systems, and identify one scenario where it would still be vulnerable to replay attacks.
Explain what multi-factor authentication (MFA) is and why it significantly increases security compared to single-factor methods. Discuss the trade-off between security and usability.

A web application uses time-based one-time passwords (TOTP) as an MFA method. An attacker compromises the user's password but cannot access the user's phone. Explain what additional attack vectors the attacker might exploit to bypass TOTP authentication, and suggest one mitigation for each.

Discuss the risks of using biometric authentication (e.g. fingerprint, facial recognition) in high-security applications. Consider both technical vulnerabilities (e.g. spoofing, false acceptance rate) and privacy/revocation issues. How do these risks compare to password-based authentication?
Define the concept of a salt in password storage. Explain why salting prevents rainbow table attacks but does not prevent dictionary attacks.

A developer uses SHA-256 to hash passwords before storage. Explain why this is inadequate, and recommend a suitable password hashing function with justification. What property of this function makes it superior for password storage?

Define what is meant by a dictionary attack on password hashes. Explain why offline dictionary attacks are considered more dangerous than online attacks, and identify two practical mitigation strategies used in modern systems.

Describe the role of a salt in password hashing. Explain how salting prevents rainbow table attacks and derive the storage requirement (in GB) needed to build a rainbow table for 8-character passwords using the full ASCII printable character set (95 characters) without salting, assuming SHA-256 digests (32 bytes each) and a table compression ratio of 1:100.

A system uses bcrypt with cost parameter c=12. Explain what the cost parameter controls and estimate the time required to hash a single password on a modern CPU. Why does this make bcrypt more resistant to brute-force attacks than unsalted SHA-256?
Explain the difference between authentication and authorisation. Give an example of a system where both can fail independently, and describe the security consequences in each case.
Define what is meant by a credential stuffing attack. How does the use of unique passwords across different services mitigate this threat? What role might a password manager play?

Discuss the human factors that lead users to choose weak passwords despite security warnings. Propose two design interventions that could encourage stronger password selection without significantly reducing usability.
Define the concept of a salt in password storage. Explain why salts are necessary and what security property they provide against dictionary and rainbow table attacks.

Consider a password hashing scheme using PBKDF2 with parameters: hash function H = SHA-256, iterations c = 100,000, salt length = 16 bytes, output length = 32 bytes. Describe the algorithm for verifying a user-supplied password against a stored hash. Why is the iteration count c important?

An attacker obtains a database of 10,000 password hashes salted with unique 16-byte random values. Explain why precomputed hash tables (rainbow tables) are ineffective against this database, and estimate the computational cost to crack one password via brute force assuming the attacker can perform 10^10 hash operations per second.
Define session hijacking and explain two distinct mechanisms by which an attacker can obtain or forge a valid session token in a web application. For each mechanism, describe one defence.
Contrast stateful (server-side session) and stateless (JWT-based) session management. For each approach, identify one significant security advantage and one significant drawback.
