With the aid of a diagram showing window size over time, describe how CUBIC's congestion window evolution differs from classic AIMD TCP, particularly in the period following a congestion event. What is the primary advantage of CUBIC's approach?

Consider two TCP flows: one using classic AIMD and one using CUBIC. Both flows share a 10 Gbit/s link with an RTT of 500 ms. Explain why CUBIC is expected to achieve higher throughput than AIMD on this link, with reference to the congestion window dynamics.

Define the congestion window (cwnd) in TCP and explain how it is used to control the rate of data transmission. In your answer, describe the relationship between cwnd and the sender's transmission rate, and explain why TCP cannot simply set cwnd to the maximum possible value.

With reference to a time-sequence diagram showing window size evolution, describe the behaviour of TCP Reno during the congestion avoidance phase. Explain what triggers the transition from congestion avoidance to fast recovery, and quantify the changes to cwnd and ssthresh during a congestion event.

TCP Reno uses multiplicative decrease (cwnd ← cwnd/2) upon detecting packet loss. Explain why this aggressive reduction is necessary, and discuss what problem might arise if TCP instead used additive decrease (cwnd ← cwnd − 1).

Modern high-speed networks often use alternative congestion control algorithms such as BBR (Bottleneck Bandwidth and RTT). Explain why classic TCP congestion control (such as Reno) may be inefficient on networks with large bandwidth-delay products. Give an example of such a network scenario with realistic parameters.

Define the concept of congestion window (cwnd) in TCP and explain how it is used to control the rate at which a sender transmits data. In your answer, distinguish between the roles of cwnd and the receiver's advertised window (rwnd).

Explain the slow start phase of TCP congestion control. Why is this phase called 'slow start' when the congestion window grows exponentially? Give an example of how cwnd evolves over the first four RTTs, assuming an initial cwnd of 1 MSS and no packet loss.

With the aid of a diagram showing congestion window size over time, compare the congestion avoidance and fast recovery phases of TCP Reno. In particular, explain what triggers the transition between these phases and how the window size changes in each phase.

A TCP sender experiences a timeout event (indicating severe congestion). Describe the immediate actions taken by TCP following a timeout and explain why these actions are more aggressive than those taken during fast retransmit.
Define the concept of a 'TCP friendly' flow. Why is it important that new congestion control algorithms are TCP friendly? Give one example of a scenario where TCP friendliness might be problematic.

Define the concept of slow start in TCP congestion control. Explain how the congestion window (cwnd) evolves during this phase, and describe what event causes TCP to exit slow start and enter congestion avoidance. Provide a simple numerical example showing cwnd growth over successive round-trip times (RTTs), assuming an initial cwnd of 1 Maximum Segment Size (MSS).

A TCP connection experiences three consecutive packet losses due to congestion, occurring at cwnd values of 32 MSS, 16 MSS, and 8 MSS respectively. Explain how classic AIMD (Additive Increase, Multiplicative Decrease) TCP congestion control responds to each loss event, showing the resulting cwnd values after each event. Assume a multiplicative decrease factor of 0.5.

With the aid of a diagram showing congestion window size against time, compare how Reno and NewReno TCP variants differ in their response to packet loss. In particular, explain the concept of 'fast recovery' and describe how NewReno improves upon Reno's handling of multiple packet losses within a single congestion window.

Consider a long-lived TCP flow operating over a high-speed network link (10 Gbit/s) with a round-trip time of 50 ms. Calculate the bandwidth-delay product (BDP) in bytes. Explain why classic AIMD congestion control performs poorly on such links, and discuss one technique (other than changing the congestion control algorithm) that could mitigate this problem.

Define the concept of congestion window (cwnd) in TCP and explain how it relates to the maximum amount of data a sender can transmit. In your answer, distinguish between cwnd and the receiver's advertised window (rwnd).

With the aid of a time-sequence diagram, describe the behaviour of classic AIMD (Additive Increase, Multiplicative Decrease) TCP congestion control during the congestion avoidance phase. Include in your explanation: (i) how the congestion window changes over time when no loss occurs, (ii) how the window responds to packet loss, and (iii) the purpose of this control mechanism.

TCP uses duplicate acknowledgements (duplicate ACKs) to detect packet loss. Explain what constitutes a duplicate ACK and why a sender typically waits to receive three duplicate ACKs before triggering fast retransmit, rather than retransmitting immediately upon receiving a single duplicate ACK.

Compare how TCP Reno and TCP Tahoe differ in their response to packet loss detection via duplicate ACKs. In particular, discuss the concept of fast recovery and explain why TCP Reno is generally preferred in modern networks.

Define the concept of congestion control in TCP. Explain why congestion control is necessary in modern networks and describe the key difference between loss-based and delay-based congestion control algorithms.

With the aid of a diagram showing congestion window (cwnd) size over time, describe the Additive Increase Multiplicative Decrease (AIMD) algorithm used in classic TCP Reno. Include in your explanation: (i) the behaviour during congestion avoidance phase, (ii) the response to packet loss, and (iii) typical values for the increase and decrease parameters.

TCP Reno's AIMD approach can lead to poor utilisation of high-bandwidth, high-latency links (large bandwidth-delay product networks). Explain why this is the case and identify what metric determines how quickly a flow can increase its sending rate.

BBR (Bottleneck Bandwidth and RTT) is a modern congestion control algorithm that differs fundamentally from loss-based approaches. Explain what BBR attempts to measure and how this differs from the assumptions made by TCP Reno. How does BBR aim to maintain a target amount of 'in-flight' data?

parts.label: ai
parts.text: A rod has endpoints x_0 and x_1 with initial orientations (rotations from a reference frame) q_0 and q_1 respectively. Consider shortest-path SO(3) interpolation of rotation along the rod. Write the quaternion for rotation θ ≤ π at x_1.

parts.label: aii
parts.text: For the rod with quaternions q_0 at x_0 and q_1 at x_1, write the quaternion at any point on the rod/time in [0,1] using shortest-path SO(3) interpolation over both time and space.

parts.label: aiii
parts.text: For the same rod, write the quaternion at any point using linear quaternion blending along the rod (spatial interpolation) combined with shortest-path SO(3) interpolation over time.

parts.label: aiv
parts.text: For the linear quaternion blending approach from part (a)(iii), write an expression for the norm of the resulting quaternion.

parts.label: bi
parts.text: A triangle has vertices v_0, v_1, v_2 in 3D. What condition must the vertices satisfy to define a valid plane?

parts.label: bii
parts.text: Write the parametric form p(u,v) for the plane defined by the triangle with vertices v_0, v_1, v_2.

parts.label: biii
parts.text: Write an expression for the plane normal defined by the triangle.

parts.label: biv
parts.text: Describe an algorithm to find the closest point on a triangle (with vertices v_0, v_1, v_2) to an arbitrary point x in 3D space.

parts.label: ai
parts.text: Consider ray tracing a scene where only the camera moves (scene geometry and lights are static), using local illumination with a single BRDF. Is pre-computation of any quantities possible, and if so, how would you use it?

parts.label: aii
parts.text: Consider ray tracing a scene where only the camera moves, using global illumination with purely diffuse reflection. Is pre-computation of any quantities possible, and if so, how would you use it?

parts.label: bi
parts.text: In path tracing with importance sampling using exact incoming radiance: write the approximation for outgoing radiance with N samples, give the light path length, and explain why the path length is finite.

parts.label: bii
parts.text: For a scene with a single point light, paths of length 3, and no shadow rays: describe the importance-sampling strategy at each intersection point.

parts.label: ci
parts.text: The rendering equation is reduced to L_o(x, ω_o) = c ∫ V(x,ω_i) cosθ_i dω_i. What scene properties allow this reduction from the full rendering equation?

parts.label: cii
parts.text: For a scene with a single object and no inter-reflections, and the reduced rendering equation L_o(x, ω_o) = c ∫ V(x,ω_i) cosθ_i dω_i, write the gradient of L_o with respect to the scene point x.

parts.label: a
parts.text: Provide quantitative estimates for: (i) the information content of English text, and (ii) the theoretical rate of information entry from a keyboard.

parts.label: b
parts.text: How could you use the relationship between information content of text and keyboard entry rate in a design to allow users to enter text faster or with less effort?

parts.label: c
parts.text: A probabilistic command-line interpreter uses Bayes' theorem to infer the user's intended command. Explain in this context what each of the following represents: Posterior probability, Likelihood, Prior probability, and Evidence.

parts.label: d
parts.text: Identify one well-known cognitive bias or heuristic applied by users of a command-line interface for making rapid decisions. Explain its effectiveness using Bayes' theorem or information theory.

parts.label: e
parts.text: Apply the attention investment model of abstraction to explain the trade-off between abstract and concrete strategies in using a probabilistic command-line interpreter.

parts.label: a
parts.text: A radio station wants an online tool using a generative AI LLM to help produce scripts — as an alternative to a conversational interface like ChatGPT. Sketch a user interface as a front end to the LLM.

parts.label: b
parts.text: Label two specific parts of your UI sketch and explain how the graphical/visual properties you chose correspond to the intended function of those elements.

parts.label: c
parts.text: Name an analytic method you could use to predict whether your designed UI would be more time-efficient than using ChatGPT directly.

parts.label: d
parts.text: Perform calculations comparing (i) ChatGPT and (ii) your UI design for typical radio script production usage. Your answers should be correct within an order of magnitude.

parts.label: e
parts.text: Describe an empirical approach to evaluate whether your calculated time-efficiency estimates are justified in practice.

parts.label: f
parts.text: Identify up to four further considerations beyond time-efficiency that are relevant to this design, with reference to specific HCI literature or frameworks.

unix-tools._note: Unix Tools is assessed by coursework only — no past paper exam questions exist for this course.
